title: R and QGIS
text:
R and QGIS are complementary tools, and many practitioners use both in the same workflow. QGIS excels at interactive, exploratory work: visually inspecting data, digitising features, and quick one-off queries. R excels at reproducible, scripted analysis that can be run end-to-end by anyone with the same data. There is no need to choose between them.
This chapter covers three ways to connect the two: simple file exchange, calling QGIS processing algorithms directly from R, and accessing GRASS GIS through R.
# The shared foundation: GDAL
R and QGIS are both built on GDAL, the Geospatial Data Abstraction Library. This means they can read and write the same file formats, and a file written by one can always be opened by the other. You do not need any special integration layer to move data between them — a GeoPackage is a GeoPackage whether you created it in R or in QGIS.
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(terra)
# File exchange
The simplest and most reliable way to combine R and QGIS in a workflow is to write files from R that QGIS can open, and to read files prepared in QGIS back into R.
GeoPackage (.gpkg) is the recommended format for this. It is an open standard supported natively by both tools, stores multiple layers in a single file, and avoids the limitations of shapefiles. GeoTIFF (.tif) is the equivalent for rasters.

title: R and QGIS
section: Writing from R for use in QGIS
text:
Writing from R for use in QGIS

# Write a processed sf object — open this directly in QGIS
write_sf(my_sf, "output/survey_results.gpkg")

# Write a raster — open in QGIS as a layer
writeRaster(my_raster, "output/interpolated_temp.tif",
            overwrite = TRUE)

# A GeoPackage can hold multiple layers
write_sf(stations_sf, "output/survey.gpkg", layer = "stations")
write_sf(tracks_sf,   "output/survey.gpkg", layer = "tracks")

title: R and QGIS
text:
Reading into R from QGIS

# Read a layer prepared or digitised in QGIS
study_areas <- read_sf("qgis_work/study_areas.gpkg")

# If the GeoPackage has multiple layers, inspect first
st_layers("qgis_work/study_areas.gpkg")

# Then read the layer you need
exclusion_zones <- read_sf("qgis_work/study_areas.gpkg",
                           layer = "exclusion_zones")







TipOrganise your project folders clearly



A simple convention that works well: keep a qgis/ folder for layers you create or edit interactively in QGIS, and an output/ folder for layers produced by your R scripts. Reading from qgis/ and writing to output/ keeps the direction of data flow obvious.



# Calling QGIS algorithms from R: qgisprocess
The qgisprocess package (Dunnington et al.) lets you run QGIS processing algorithms directly from an R script, passing sf and terra objects as inputs and getting results back as R objects. This is the current recommended approach for tight R–QGIS integration.
It requires a local QGIS installation (version 3.14 or later), and it wraps the qgis_process command-line tool that QGIS provides. All providers available in your QGIS installation are accessible: native QGIS algorithms, GDAL, GRASS, and SAGA.

# install.packages("qgisprocess")
library(qgisprocess)

title: R and QGIS
text:
Checking your setup

# Verify that qgisprocess can find your QGIS installation
qgis_path()
qgis_version()

title: R and QGIS
text:
Discovering available algorithms

# List all available algorithms
qgis_algorithms()

# Search by keyword
qgis_algorithms() |>
  filter(grepl("buffer", algorithm, ignore.case = TRUE)) |>
  select(provider, algorithm, algorithm_title)

# Get the full documentation for one algorithm
qgis_show_help("native:buffer")

title: R and QGIS
section: A worked example: buffering survey stations
text:
A worked example: buffering survey stations

# Load survey stations
stations <- read_sf("./data/is_smb_stations.csv") |>
  # (already an sf object with geometry)
  st_transform(3057)   # reproject to ISN93 (metres) for buffering

# Run the QGIS buffer algorithm
result <- qgis_run_algorithm(
  algorithm = "native:buffer",
  INPUT     = stations,
  DISTANCE  = 5000,    # 5 km buffer, in the CRS units (metres)
  SEGMENTS  = 16,
  DISSOLVE  = FALSE
)

# Convert the output back to an sf object
station_buffers <- sf::read_sf(qgis_output(result, "OUTPUT"))

# Inspect
glimpse(station_buffers)







Note



The DISTANCE parameter is always in the units of the input layer’s CRS. Reproject to a projected CRS (metres) before buffering if your data are in degrees.

title: R and QGIS
section: Another example: dissolving polygons by attribute
text:
Another example: dissolving polygons by attribute

eez <- read_sf("./data/eez.gpkg")

result <- qgis_run_algorithm(
  algorithm = "native:dissolve",
  INPUT     = eez,
  FIELD     = "country"
)

eez_dissolved <- sf::read_sf(qgis_output(result, "OUTPUT"))

title: R and QGIS
text:
Chaining algorithms
qgisprocess results can be passed directly as inputs to the next algorithm without converting back to sf at each step:

# Buffer, then dissolve, in one chain
buffered <- qgis_run_algorithm("native:buffer",
                                INPUT    = stations,
                                DISTANCE = 5000)

dissolved <- qgis_run_algorithm("native:dissolve",
                                 INPUT = qgis_output(buffered, "OUTPUT"))

final <- sf::read_sf(qgis_output(dissolved, "OUTPUT"))







WarningPackage versions



qgisprocess stabilised on CRAN in 2023 and is the successor to the older RQGIS and RQGIS3 packages, which are no longer maintained. If you encounter references to RQGIS in older tutorials or Stack Overflow answers, use qgisprocess instead.
Check the current version and documentation at: https://r-spatial.github.io/qgisprocess/

title: Rasters
text:
Packages needed for this tutorial:

library(tidyverse)   # installs sweeps of packages
library(sf)          # working with sf objects in R
library(terra)       # working with rasters in R (replaces raster/rgdal)
library(tidyterra)   # ggplot2 integration for terra objects
library(marmap)      # access global topography data







Note



This chapter uses terra, which replaced the older raster and rgdal packages. The API is similar – differences are noted throughout. For the full background, see the Spatial data concepts chapter.

title: Rasters
text:
Extracting values from a raster

title: Rasters
text:
Replacing values

rst <- mintemp[20:25, 50:55, drop = FALSE]

rst[4, 2] <- rst[4, 2] + 30
rst[1:2]  <- rst[1:2]  + 10
plot(rst)

title: Rasters
text:
Masks
We can mask a raster with another raster that shares the same geometry. Cells with NA values in the mask are removed from the masked raster.

rst <- mintemp[100:135, 50:85, drop = FALSE]

# Create an empty raster with the same geometry
my.mask <- rast(rst)
values(my.mask) <- 1   # Non-NA everywhere

set.seed(100)
cells <- sample(1:ncell(my.mask), 20)
my.mask[cells] <- NA

rst_masked <- mask(rst, my.mask)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(rst)
plot(rst_masked)

title: Rasters
section: Extracting raster data with sf objects
text:
Extracting raster data with sf objects

bt_points <- read_sf("./data/ice_points.gpkg")
bt_lines  <- read_sf("./data/ice_lines.gpkg")
bt_poly   <- read_sf("./data/ice_polygon.gpkg")

plot(mintemp)
plot(vect(bt_points), add = TRUE)
plot(vect(bt_lines),  add = TRUE)
plot(vect(bt_poly),   add = TRUE)








We use terra::extract() to extract raster values using sf objects (converted to SpatVector with vect()):

For POINT geometry: values of the cells where the points are located.
For LINESTRING geometry: values of the cells touched by the line.
For POLYGON geometry: values of the cells whose centres fall within the polygon.


ex1 <- terra::extract(mintemp, vect(bt_points))  # data frame, one row per point
ex2 <- terra::extract(mintemp, vect(bt_lines))   # data frame, one row per cell touched
ex3 <- terra::extract(mintemp, vect(bt_poly))    # data frame, one row per cell

# With cell numbers
ex3_cells <- terra::extract(mintemp, vect(bt_poly), cells = TRUE)

We can also use an sf object as a cookie cutter to mask a raster with mask(). Make sure that they share the same CRS.

temp_masked     <- mask(mintemp, vect(bt_poly))
temp_masked_inv <- mask(mintemp, vect(bt_poly), inverse = TRUE)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(temp_masked)
plot(temp_masked_inv)

text:
Changing the resolution
aggregate() and disagg() change the resolution by merging or splitting cells. Note: in terra, disaggregate() is renamed disagg().

dim(agg_depth)

dis_depth <- disagg(depth, fact = 5, method = "bilinear")
dim(dis_depth)

plot(depth)

title: Rasters
text:
Cropping

ext_obj    <- ext(c(-25, -20, 63, 65))
crop_depth <- crop(depth, ext_obj)
plot(crop_depth)

title: Rasters
text:
Merging rasters

ice_temp <- mintemp + 10

# merge(): overlapping cells use values from the first raster
icefar_temp <- merge(ice_temp, far_temp)
plot(icefar_temp)







# mosaic(): overlapping cells are combined using a function
icefar_temp2 <- mosaic(ice_temp, far_temp, fun = "mean")
plot(icefar_temp2)

title: Rasters
text:
Resampling and projecting rasters
Resampling transfers values between rasters with different origin or resolution. Projecting additionally changes the coordinate reference system. In terra both are handled by resample() and project() (the latter replaces projectRaster()).

# Create a target raster with a shifted origin and finer resolution
target1 <- rast(depth)
origin(target1) <- c(0, 0)
res(target1)    <- 0.025

depth_res <- resample(depth, target1, method = "bilinear")

# Changing the CRS (replaces projectRaster())
crs(depth)

Use method = "bilinear" for continuous rasters and method = "near" (nearest neighbour) for categorical or integer rasters.

title: Working with geometries in sf objects
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
The sf package provides a large set of functions to operate on the geometries of sf objects. This chapter covers the most useful ones, organised by what they do:
We will use the Nephrops functional units as our main example dataset throughout, projected to World Mercator for accurate distance and area calculations:
nfu <- read_sf("./data/nephrops_fu.gpkg") |>
  st_transform(3395)

title: Working with geometries in sf objects
text:
Sampling
st_sample() generates random points within polygons or along linestrings. The result is an sfc object (a geometry column without attributes). Use st_as_sf() to convert it to a full sf object if you need to attach data.

set.seed(100)

# Random points within polygons
pts1 <- st_sample(nfu, size = 100, type = "random")

# Random points along the boundary of polygons
nfu_lines <- st_cast(nfu, "MULTILINESTRING")
pts2 <- st_sample(nfu_lines, size = 100, type = "random")

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = nfu, fill = "grey90") +
  geom_sf(data = pts1, colour = "red", size = 0.8) +
  geom_sf(data = pts2, colour = "blue", size = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Random points inside (red) and on boundary of (blue) polygons") +
  theme_bw()

title: Working with geometries in sf objects
text:
Centroids
st_centroid() returns the geometric centre of each feature. For irregular or concave polygons the centroid can fall outside the polygon itself. st_point_on_surface() guarantees a point that is always inside (or on the boundary of) the polygon, which is often more useful for labelling.

centr  <- st_centroid(nfu)
centr2 <- st_point_on_surface(nfu)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = nfu, fill = "grey90") +
  geom_sf(data = centr,  colour = "red",  size = 2) +
  geom_sf(data = centr2, colour = "blue", size = 2, shape = 17) +
  labs(title = "Centroids (red circles) vs point on surface (blue triangles)") +
  theme_bw()

title: Working with geometries in sf objects
text:
Buffers
st_buffer() creates a zone around a geometry at a given distance (in the units of the CRS — metres for most projected CRS). It works on points, lines, and polygons.

# Buffer around points: creates circles
buf_pts <- st_buffer(pts1, dist = 100000)  # 100 km

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = buf_pts, linetype = "dashed", fill = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = pts1, colour = "red") +
  theme_bw()









# Buffer around lines: creates a corridor
buf_lines <- st_buffer(nfu_lines, dist = 50000)  # 50 km either side

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = buf_lines, aes(fill = name), alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_sf(data = nfu, fill = NA, colour = "black") +
  theme_bw()









# Buffer around polygons: expands the polygon outward
buf_poly <- st_buffer(nfu, dist = 20000)  # 20 km outward

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = buf_poly, aes(fill = name), alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_sf(data = nfu, fill = NA, colour = "black") +
  theme_bw()









# Negative buffers shrink a polygon inward (only valid for polygons)
buf_neg <- st_buffer(nfu, dist = -20000)  # 20 km inward

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = nfu, fill = "grey90") +
  geom_sf(data = buf_neg, aes(fill = name), alpha = 0.6) +
  theme_bw()

title: Working with geometries in sf objects
text:
Convex and concave hulls
A convex hull is the smallest convex polygon that encloses a set of points — think of stretching a rubber band around the outermost points. To get a single hull for a set of points, first union them into a MULTIPOINT with st_union(), then apply st_convex_hull().

pts1_u <- st_union(pts1)        # Combine into a single MULTIPOINT
chull  <- st_convex_hull(pts1_u)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = chull, fill = "lightblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_sf(data = pts1, colour = "red", size = 0.8) +
  theme_bw()














Note



st_union() is also used more generally to dissolve internal boundaries between polygons (merging multiple polygons into one) and to cluster individual points into a MULTIPOINT geometry. Without calling st_union() first, st_convex_hull() would return a degenerate “hull” for each individual point.


A concave hull fits more tightly around a point cloud, following the actual shape of the data rather than just the outermost extent. Modern sf (version 1.0-14 and later) includes st_concave_hull() directly:

# ratio controls how tight the hull is (0 = very tight, 1 = convex hull)
conc1 <- st_concave_hull(pts1_u, ratio = 0.1)
conc2 <- st_concave_hull(pts1_u, ratio = 0.5)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = chull,  colour = "black", fill = NA, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_sf(data = conc2, colour = "blue",  fill = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = conc1, colour = "red",   fill = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = pts1, size = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Convex hull (dashed), concave ratio=0.5 (blue), ratio=0.1 (red)") +
  theme_bw()

title: Working with geometries in sf objects
text:
Grids
st_make_grid() creates rectangular or hexagonal grids over the extent of an sf object. Hexagonal grids are increasingly popular for spatial analysis because each cell has equal distance to all its neighbours.

sq_grid  <- st_make_grid(nfu, n = c(10, 15))
hex_grid <- st_make_grid(nfu, n = c(10, 15), square = FALSE)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = sq_grid,  fill = NA, colour = "grey60") +
  geom_sf(data = hex_grid, fill = NA, colour = "red", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_sf(data = nfu, fill = NA, colour = "black") +
  theme_bw()








To get just the bounding box of an sf object as a polygon, use st_as_sfc() on the bounding box:

bbox_poly <- st_as_sfc(st_bbox(nfu))

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = bbox_poly, fill = NA, colour = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_sf(data = nfu, fill = "grey90") +
  theme_bw()

title: Working with geometries in sf objects
text:
Geometry type casting
st_cast() converts geometries from one type to another. Common uses include converting POLYGON to LINESTRING (to get the boundary as a line), splitting MULTIPOLYGON into individual POLYGONs, or combining POINTs into a LINESTRING for a track.

# Convert polygons to their boundary lines
nfu_lines <- st_cast(nfu, "MULTILINESTRING")
st_geometry_type(nfu_lines)

 [1] MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING
 [5] MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING
 [9] MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING
[13] MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING
[17] MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING
[21] MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING
[25] MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING
[29] MULTILINESTRING MULTILINESTRING
18 Levels: GEOMETRY POINT LINESTRING POLYGON MULTIPOINT ... TRIANGLE

# Split MULTIPOLYGON features into individual POLYGONs
# (warning: this duplicates attribute rows for each part)
nfu_poly <- st_cast(nfu, "POLYGON")
nrow(nfu)       # Original number of features

[1] 30

nrow(nfu_poly)  # May be more if any features are multipart

[1] 30








WarningCasting from MULTI to single geometry types



When casting from MULTIPOLYGON to POLYGON (or MULTILINESTRING to LINESTRING, etc.), each part of a multi-geometry becomes a separate row. Attribute values are duplicated across all resulting rows. Be careful when doing this before area or length calculations, as you may end up double-counting.

title: Working with geometries in sf objects
text:
Set operations on geometries
These binary operations take two sf objects and return new geometries based on their spatial relationship. They are among the most powerful tools in sf.

# Load a second polygon layer to work with
ices_er <- read_sf("./data/ices_ecoregions.gpkg") |>
  st_transform(3395)

# Select just a couple of areas to keep examples clear
north_sea <- ices_er |> filter(ecoregion == "Greater North Sea")
celtic_sea <- ices_er |> filter(ecoregion == "Celtic Seas")

st_intersection() returns the overlapping part of two geometries. This is equivalent to a spatial “AND” — give me everything that is in both A and B.

# Which Nephrops functional units fall within the Greater North Sea ecoregion?
nfu_north_sea <- st_intersection(nfu, north_sea)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = north_sea, fill = "lightblue", alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = nfu_north_sea, aes(fill = name)) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "NFUs intersecting the Greater North Sea ecoregion")








st_difference() returns the part of the first geometry that does not overlap with the second — spatial “A minus B”.

# Parts of the Greater North Sea that are NOT covered by any NFU
north_sea_no_nfu <- st_difference(north_sea, st_union(nfu))

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = north_sea, fill = "grey90") +
  geom_sf(data = north_sea_no_nfu, fill = "red", alpha = 0.5) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "Parts of the Greater North Sea not covered by any NFU")








st_union() combines geometries, dissolving internal boundaries. Applied to a whole layer it returns a single merged polygon.

# Dissolve all NFUs into a single polygon
nfu_dissolved <- st_union(nfu)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = nfu_dissolved, fill = "lightgreen") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "All NFUs dissolved into one polygon")








st_sym_difference() returns everything that is in either A or B but not in both — the spatial “XOR”.






NoteA note on CRS in set operations



Both objects in a binary set operation must share the same CRS. sf will throw an error if they do not. Always check with st_crs(a) == st_crs(b) or transform one to match the other before proceeding.

title: Working with geometries in sf objects
Working with geometries in sf objects
text:
Distance
st_distance() returns a matrix of pairwise distances between two sets of geometries. Rows correspond to features in the first object, columns to features in the second. Results are units objects.

set.seed(100)
poly  <- nfu[1:3, ]
pts_a <- st_sample(poly, 5) |> st_as_sf() |> mutate(lab = 1:5)
pts_b <- st_sample(poly, 2) |> st_as_sf()

# All pairwise distances between pts_a and pts_b
st_distance(pts_a, pts_b)

Units: [m]
          [,1]      [,2]
[1,] 390038.79 215873.96
[2,] 271325.80 132231.48
[3,]  83469.62  91539.58
[4,] 413750.94 253766.06
[5,] 313926.48 141064.78

# Distance from each point to each polygon
# Note: distance is 0 when the point is inside the polygon
st_distance(pts_a, poly)

Units: [m]
         [,1]      [,2]     [,3]
[1,] 514192.8  99476.91      0.0
[2,] 357723.2  13305.68      0.0
[3,] 227285.5      0.00 184031.4
[4,] 503294.0 154725.62      0.0
[5,] 430993.2  41763.01      0.0

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = poly, aes(fill = name), alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = pts_a) +
  geom_sf_text(data = pts_a, aes(label = lab), nudge_x = 20000) +
  theme_bw()








For nearest-neighbour distances — the distance from each feature in one layer to its closest feature in another — use the by_element argument together with st_nearest_feature():

# Index of the nearest polygon for each point in pts_a
nearest_idx <- st_nearest_feature(pts_a, poly)

# Distance from each point to its nearest polygon
st_distance(pts_a, poly[nearest_idx, ], by_element = TRUE)

Units: [m]
[1] 0 0 0 0 0

title: Working with geometries in sf objects
text:
Dissolving polygon boundaries
A common task is merging polygons that share a grouping variable, dissolving the internal boundaries between them.

ices_er <- read_sf("./data/ices_ecoregions.gpkg") |>
  st_transform(3395) |>
  mutate(zone = case_when(
    ecoregion %in% c("Arctic Ocean", "Barents Sea") ~ "Arctic & Subarctic",
    ecoregion %in% c("Bay of Biscay and the Iberian Coast",
                     "Mediterranean Sea", "Black Sea") ~ "Mediterranean & Black Sea",
    TRUE ~ "North Atlantic"
  ))

ices_zones <- ices_er |> 
  group_by(zone) |> 
  summarise(area_km2 = sum(area_km2)) |>   # do_union = TRUE is the default
  st_transform(4326) # For easier plotting

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = ices_zones, aes(fill = zone), alpha = 0.7) +
  coord_sf(ylim=c(30,85)) +
  theme_bw()














Note



The default do_union = TRUE in summarise() uses st_union() to dissolve internal boundaries between merged polygons. This is what you want for polygon aggregation. Using do_union = FALSE would keep internal boundaries and can produce invalid geometries.

title: Working with geometries in sf objects
text:
Combining sf objects by row
To stack two sf objects with the same columns and CRS, use bind_rows() (tidyverse) or rbind() (base R):

sm_ices_er <- ices_er |> filter(area_km2 <  500)
lg_ices_er <- ices_er |> filter(area_km2 >= 500)

all_ices_er <- bind_rows(sm_ices_er, lg_ices_er)
nrow(all_ices_er) == nrow(ices_er)  # Should be TRUE

[1] TRUE

title: Coordinate Reference Systems
text:
A fundamental aspect of spatial data is defining its Coordinate Reference System (CRS). A CRS defines how coordinates relate to actual locations on the Earth’s surface. Without a CRS, coordinates are just numbers — a latitude of 65 and a longitude of -18 mean nothing unless we know which CRS they are expressed in.
A CRS is built from three components: an ellipsoid, a datum, and a projection.

title: Coordinate Reference Systems
text:
Ellipsoid
The Earth is not a perfect sphere — it bulges at the equator and is flattened at the poles, and its surface is irregular. An ellipsoid is a smooth mathematical model that approximates the Earth’s shape.
Some ellipsoids are fitted globally, trying to minimise error across the whole planet. WGS84 is the most widely used global ellipsoid — it underpins GPS and most modern datasets. GRS80 is nearly identical and underlies many national systems.
Local ellipsoids are fitted to a specific region and can be more accurate there. Examples include ED50 (fitted to Europe) and OSGB36 (Great Britain). You may encounter these in older national datasets.

title: Coordinate Reference Systems
text:
Datum
The datum anchors the ellipsoid to the actual surface of the Earth by defining an origin point and the orientation of the coordinate axes. The datum always specifies which ellipsoid it uses, and sometimes shares its name (e.g. WGS84 is both an ellipsoid and a datum).
Importantly, the same latitude/longitude values can refer to different physical locations depending on which datum is used. For most marine science work the differences are small enough to ignore, but they matter for precise navigation or cadastral work.
When the datum is unknown, it is generally safe to assume WGS84.

title: Coordinate Reference Systems
text:
Projection
Finally, the projection specifies how we represent the curved surface of the Earth on a flat plane. There are two broad approaches:
a) Geographic coordinates (unprojected): Latitude and longitude are spherical coordinates that describe positions on the 3D ellipsoid. When plotted directly on screen, they are treated as Cartesian (x, y) pairs — this is an implicit Equidistant Cylindrical projection and is fine for many purposes but distorts distances and areas, especially at high latitudes.









b) Projected coordinates: An explicit mathematical transformation converts spherical coordinates into planar (Cartesian) coordinates in metres (or feet). Projected CRS are essential for accurate distance and area calculations, and for geometric operations.

title: Coordinate Reference Systems
text:
How to specify a CRS
There are two main ways to specify a CRS in sf:
1. EPSG codes — the preferred approach for common CRS. These are integer codes from the EPSG Geodetic Parameter Dataset that identify a specific CRS unambiguously. You can refer to them just by their number, but it is better to use the full “authority:code” notation.

# EPSG:4326  — geographic coordinates, WGS84 (the GPS standard)
# EPSG:32628 — UTM zone 28N, WGS84
# EPSG:3035  — ETRS89-LAEA, the standard equal-area CRS for Europe
# EPSG:8088  — ISN2016 / LAEA Iceland (Iceland's official CRS)
# 8088  — same as above, but it is better to use the full notation
# ESRI:54009  — Mollweide projection — only exists in the ESRI registry, not EPSG

2. Proj4 strings (legacy) — strings like "+proj=longlat +datum=WGS84". These still work for basic cases but are deprecated in modern PROJ. They cannot represent all CRS parameters and may produce warnings. You will encounter them in older code and documentation; prefer EPSG codes for new work.

title: Coordinate Reference Systems
section: Querying the CRS of an object
text:
Querying the CRS of an object

library(rnaturalearth)
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)

iceland <- ne_countries(returnclass = "sf", country = "Iceland", scale = 10) |>
  st_geometry()

st_crs(iceland)        # Full CRS information (WKT2 representation)

Coordinate Reference System:
  User input: WGS 84 
  wkt:
GEOGCRS["WGS 84",
    DATUM["World Geodetic System 1984",
        ELLIPSOID["WGS 84",6378137,298.257223563,
            LENGTHUNIT["metre",1]]],
    PRIMEM["Greenwich",0,
        ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433]],
    CS[ellipsoidal,2],
        AXIS["latitude",north,
            ORDER[1],
            ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433]],
        AXIS["longitude",east,
            ORDER[2],
            ANGLEUNIT["degree",0.0174532925199433]],
    ID["EPSG",4326]]

st_crs(iceland)$epsg   # Just the EPSG code

[1] 4326

st_crs(iceland)$Name   # Human-readable name

[1] "WGS 84"

st_is_longlat(iceland) # Is it geographic (unprojected)?

[1] TRUE

title: Coordinate Reference Systems
section: Setting vs. transforming: a critical distinction
text:
Setting vs. transforming: a critical distinction
There are two very different operations that both involve CRS, and confusing them is one of the most common mistakes in spatial analysis:






ImportantSetting a CRS vs. transforming a CRS



Setting (st_set_crs()) tells R what coordinate system the data is already in. It only changes the metadata — the coordinate values themselves do not change. Use this when you have data without CRS information attached.
Transforming (st_transform()) actually recalculates all coordinates from one CRS to another. Use this when you want to change the projection.
Applying st_set_crs() to data that is already correctly tagged will produce a warning and give wrong results. Applying st_transform() to data with no CRS will fail.



# Creating an sf object without a CRS
mysf <- data.frame(lat = c(66, 65.5), lon = c(-20, -19)) |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("lon", "lat"))

st_crs(mysf) # NA — no CRS defined

Coordinate Reference System: NA

# STEP 1: Set the CRS (we know these are WGS84 lat/lon)
mysf <- mysf |>
  st_set_crs(4326)

st_crs(mysf)$Name # Now it has a CRS

[1] "WGS 84"

# STEP 2: Transform to a projected CRS (LAEA Iceland)
mysf_projected <- mysf |>
  st_transform(8088)

st_crs(mysf_projected)$Name

[1] "ISN2016 / Lambert 2016"

st_is_longlat(mysf_projected) # FALSE — now it's projected

[1] FALSE


Calling st_set_crs() on an object that already has a CRS will produce a warning — this is intentional, to guard against accidentally overwriting correct metadata:

# This produces a warning — the CRS is being replaced, not transformed
st_set_crs(mysf, 32628)

Simple feature collection with 2 features and 0 fields
Geometry type: POINT
Dimension:     XY
Bounding box:  xmin: -20 ymin: 65.5 xmax: -19 ymax: 66
Projected CRS: WGS 84 / UTM zone 28N
          geometry
1   POINT (-20 66)
2 POINT (-19 65.5)

title: Coordinate Reference Systems
section: Finding the right CRS with crsuggest
text:
Finding the right CRS with crsuggest
The crsuggest package can help you identify appropriate projected CRS options for your data based on its geographic extent:

library(crsuggest)

suggest_crs(iceland)        # Returns a ranked list of suitable CRS options

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(rnaturalearthdata)
library(ggspatial)
library(patchwork)





minke_df <- read_csv("data/minke.csv")
smb      <- read_csv("data/smb_summary.csv")

# Convert minke whale positions to sf
minke_sf <- minke_df |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326)

# Country polygons from rnaturalearth
iceland_sf <- ne_countries(country = "Iceland", scale = "medium")
world_sf   <- ne_countries(scale = "medium")

# Nephrops functional units — used for labelling examples
nfu <- read_sf("data/nephrops_fu.gpkg")

# Iceland coastline and depth contours — used in the mixed-CRS example
coast    <- read_sf("data/iceland_coastline.gpkg")
contours <- read_sf("data/iceland_contours.gpkg")

# SMB 2019 tows, as both a data frame and an sf object
smb2019     <- smb |> filter(year == 2019)
smb2019_pts <- smb2019 |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("lon1", "lat1"), crs = 4326)

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(rnaturalearthdata)
library(ggspatial)
library(patchwork)

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
text:
minke_df <- read_csv("data/minke.csv")
smb      <- read_csv("data/smb_summary.csv")

# Convert minke whale positions to sf
minke_sf <- minke_df |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326)

# Country polygons from rnaturalearth
iceland_sf <- ne_countries(country = "Iceland", scale = "medium")
world_sf   <- ne_countries(scale = "medium")

# Nephrops functional units — used for labelling examples
nfu <- read_sf("data/nephrops_fu.gpkg")

# Iceland coastline and depth contours — used in the mixed-CRS example
coast    <- read_sf("data/iceland_coastline.gpkg")
contours <- read_sf("data/iceland_contours.gpkg")

# SMB 2019 tows, as both a data frame and an sf object
smb2019     <- smb |> filter(year == 2019)
smb2019_pts <- smb2019 |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("lon1", "lat1"), crs = 4326)

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
text:
Mixing layers with different CRS
When layers come from different sources with different CRS, coord_sf() aligns them automatically. Without an explicit crs argument it uses the CRS of the first layer, which can produce unexpected results if the layer order changes. Setting it explicitly is safer:

# coast: Iceland coastline in a projected LAEA CRS
# contours: depth contours in WGS84 (EPSG:4326)
# — these would not align without coord_sf() handling the reprojection

# Without explicit CRS — display CRS taken from first layer (contours = WGS84)
p_implicit <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = contours, colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = coast, fill = "grey80") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "Implicit CRS (first layer wins)", subtitle = "WGS84")

# With explicit CRS — ISN2016 / LAEA Iceland (Iceland's official CRS)
p_explicit <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = contours, colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = coast, fill = "grey80") +
  coord_sf(crs = 8088) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "Explicit CRS", subtitle = "ISN2016 / LAEA Iceland (EPSG:8088)")

p_implicit + p_explicit














Important



Always set crs explicitly in coord_sf() when combining layers from different sources. The implicit default can change silently if you reorder your layers.

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
text:
Continuous data: viridis palettes
The viridis palettes ("viridis", "magma", "plasma", "inferno", "cividis", "mako", "rocket", "turbo") are built into ggplot2. They are perceptually uniform, readable in greyscale, and colour-blind safe — a reliable default for scientific maps:

s <- smb |>
  filter(year == 2019) |>
  group_by(ir_lon, ir_lat) |>
  summarise(Cod = mean(cod_n), .groups = "drop") |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("ir_lon", "ir_lat"), crs = 4326)

p_raster <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = s, aes(colour = Cod), size = 3) +
  geom_sf(data = iceland_sf, fill = "grey60", colour = NA) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-27, -11), ylim = c(62.5, 67.5)) +
  scale_x_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  theme_bw()

p1 <- p_raster + scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "D", name = "viridis")
p2 <- p_raster + scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "B", direction = -1,
                                         name = "magma (rev)")
p3 <- p_raster + scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "C", name = "plasma")

p1 + p2 + p3

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
text:
Colour scale transformations
Catch data are strongly right-skewed: most tows catch few fish and a few catch a lot. On an untransformed scale, almost all the colour gradient is consumed by the rare high values, obscuring variation among typical tows. Transforming the scale spreads the gradient more evenly:

p_log  <- p_raster +
  scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "B", direction = -1,
                         transform = "log1p", name = "log1p")
p_sqrt <- p_raster +
  scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "B", direction = -1,
                         transform = "sqrt", name = "sqrt")

p_log + p_sqrt








log1p (log(x + 1)) handles zeros and compresses high values strongly. sqrt is a milder compression. Both typically reveal spatial structure that a linear scale hides.

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
text:
Discrete intervals with cut()
Binning into labelled classes trades precision for legibility — often the right choice for reports or presentations:

s_cut <- s |>
  mutate(Cod_class = cut(Cod,
                         breaks = c(0, 23, 62, 145, Inf),
                         labels = c("Low", "Medium", "High", "Very high"),
                         include.lowest = TRUE))

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = s_cut, aes(colour = Cod_class), size = 3) +
  geom_sf(data = iceland_sf, fill = "grey60", colour = NA) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-27, -11), ylim = c(62.5, 67.5)) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "B", direction = -1,
                         name = "Cod abundance",
                         na.value = "grey80") +
  scale_x_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "Cod abundance classes — SMB 2019")














NoteWhich colour scale to choose?










Situation
Recommended approach




Precise values matter, data roughly symmetric
Continuous gradient


Data are strongly right-skewed (e.g. catch counts)
Continuous with transform = "log1p" or "sqrt"


Communicating to a non-technical audience or for a report
Discrete bins with cut()

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
text:
facet_wrap()
facet_wrap() arranges panels in a ribbon, filling rows left to right. Here we convert the 2019 SMB tows to sf and map cod abundance for six survey years:

smb_sel_sf <- smb |>
  filter(year %in% c(1995, 2000, 2005, 2010, 2015, 2019)) |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("lon1", "lat1"), crs = 4326)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = iceland_sf, fill = "grey80") +
  geom_sf(data = smb_sel_sf, aes(size = cod_n),
          alpha = 0.15, colour = "red") +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 12, name = "Cod (n)") +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-27, -11), ylim = c(62.5, 67.5)) +
  scale_x_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  facet_wrap(~ year) +
  theme_bw()

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
text:
facet_grid()
facet_grid(rows ~ cols) creates a matrix of panels. This is useful for comparing two variables simultaneously — here species and year. We pivot to long format first so both species can act as a facet variable:

smb_long_sf <- smb |>
  filter(year %in% c(2005, 2010, 2015, 2019)) |>
  pivot_longer(cols      = c(cod_n, haddock_n),
               names_to  = "species",
               values_to = "n") |>
  mutate(species = case_match(species,
                              "cod_n"     ~ "Cod",
                              "haddock_n" ~ "Haddock")) |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("lon1", "lat1"), crs = 4326)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = iceland_sf, fill = "grey80") +
  geom_sf(data = smb_long_sf, aes(size = n),
          alpha = 0.2, colour = "red") +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 8, name = "Catch (n)") +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-27, -11), ylim = c(62.5, 67.5)) +
  scale_x_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, NULL) +
  facet_grid(species ~ year) +
  theme_bw()








In facet_grid(rows ~ cols), the first variable defines rows and the second defines columns. Use . ~ year to facet columns only, or species ~ . for rows only. Note the . is a placeholder meaning “no variable”.






TipExercise



Add saithe_n as a third species to the pivot_longer() call. Does the three-row grid remain legible, or would facet_wrap() be a better choice here?

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
text:
Geographic degree labels
coord_sf() formats axis labels automatically with degree symbols and cardinal direction suffixes (e.g. 25°W, 63°N). However, it delegates break placement to sf::st_graticule() internally and largely ignores scale_x_continuous(breaks = ...), so there is no direct way to control where tick marks fall from within coord_sf(). The helper functions below work around this by overriding the scale layer directly, giving precise control over break positions:

scale_longitude <- function(min = -180, max = 180, step = 1, ...) {
  breaks <- seq(min, max, step)
  labels <- ifelse(breaks < 0,
                   paste0(abs(breaks), "\u00B0W"),
                   paste0(breaks,      "\u00B0E"))
  scale_x_continuous(name = NULL, breaks = breaks, labels = labels, ...)
}

scale_latitude <- function(min = -90, max = 90, step = 0.5, ...) {
  breaks <- seq(min, max, step)
  labels <- ifelse(breaks < 0,
                   paste0(abs(breaks), "\u00B0S"),
                   paste0(breaks,      "\u00B0N"))
  scale_y_continuous(name = NULL, breaks = breaks, labels = labels, ...)
}

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = iceland_sf, fill = "grey80") +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-25, -12), ylim = c(63, 67)) +
  scale_longitude(min = -25, max = -12, step = 2) +
  scale_latitude( min =  63, max =  67, step = 1) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "Controlled degree-label breaks")














Note



If the automatic break placement looks fine, you can suppress axis labels entirely with scale_x_continuous(NULL, NULL) and scale_y_continuous(NULL, NULL), which is what the facet examples above do to save space.

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
section: Scale bars and north arrows with ggspatial
text:
Scale bars and north arrows with ggspatial
The ggspatial package adds cartographic annotations to geom_sf() maps. annotation_scale() draws a scale bar that updates automatically when you zoom; annotation_north_arrow() adds a north arrow:

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = iceland_sf, fill = "grey80") +
  annotation_scale(location = "bl", width_hint = 0.4) +
  annotation_north_arrow(
    location   = "bl",
    which_north = "true",
    pad_x      = unit(0.6, "in"),
    pad_y      = unit(0.4, "in"),
    style      = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering
  ) +
  coord_sf() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "Scale bar and north arrow")

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
text:
Themes for maps
theme_bw() is a good general-purpose theme that keeps axis labels and grid lines, which aid spatial orientation. theme_void() strips everything — axes, grid lines, and background — producing a cleaner result for publication figures that already carry a scale bar and north arrow as their own spatial reference:

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = iceland_sf, fill = "grey85", colour = "white") +
  geom_sf(data = minke_sf, aes(colour = sex), size = 1.8, alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_brewer(name = "Sex", palette = "Set1") +
  annotation_scale(location = "br") +
  annotation_north_arrow(
    location = "tl",
    style    = north_arrow_minimal,
    height   = unit(0.8, "cm"),
    width    = unit(0.8, "cm")
  ) +
  coord_sf() +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position          = "bottom",
        plot.title               = element_text(face = "bold")) +
  labs(title   = "Minke whale sightings",
       caption = "Source: MFRI")








Use theme_void() when the map is the entire figure and spatial context comes from its own annotations. Use theme_bw() when the map sits alongside other plots or when axis labels help readers orient themselves.

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
text:
Inset maps with inset_element()
inset_element() from patchwork places one plot inside another — useful for a small context map inside a detailed regional map. Here we derive the bounding box of the detail map programmatically so the red rectangle always stays in sync:

# Define the extent of the detail map explicitly so we can reuse it
detail_xlim <- c(-27, -11)
detail_ylim <- c(62.5, 67.5)

context_map <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = world_sf, fill = "grey80", colour = "white",
          linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_rect(aes(xmin = detail_xlim[1], xmax = detail_xlim[2],
                ymin = detail_ylim[1], ymax = detail_ylim[2]),
            fill = NA, colour = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-40, 30), ylim = c(45, 80)) +
  theme_void()

detail_map <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = iceland_sf, fill = "grey80") +
  geom_sf(data = minke_sf, aes(colour = sex), alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_brewer(name = "Sex", palette = "Set1") +
  coord_sf(xlim = detail_xlim, ylim = detail_ylim) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "Minke whale sightings")

detail_map +
  inset_element(context_map,
                left   = 0.65, right  = 1.0,
                bottom = 0.0,  top    = 0.35)














TipExercise



Move the inset to the top-left corner by adjusting the left, right, bottom, and top arguments. Then try making the context map show a wider extent (e.g. the full North Atlantic) and see whether the inset remains informative at that scale.

title: Mapping with ggplot2 — part 2
text:
Comparing two effort metrics
SurfSAR is normalised by cell area; KWfishingH weights fishing hours by vessel engine power. Placing them side by side with patchwork lets you compare their spatial patterns directly:

p_sar <- ggplot(ospar_int) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = SurfSAR), colour = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option    = "viridis",
                       direction = -1,
                       transform = "log1p",
                       name      = "SurfSAR (log)") +
  labs(title = "Surface swept area ratio") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text  = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank())

p_kw <- ggplot(ospar_int) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = KWfishingH), colour = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option    = "viridis",
                       direction = -1,
                       transform = "log1p",
                       name      = "kW·h (log)") +
  labs(title = "Effort (kW-fishing hours)") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text  = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank())

p_sar + p_kw +
  plot_annotation(
    title   = "Otter trawl fishing intensity — North-East Atlantic, 2015",
    caption = "Source: OSPAR Commission",
    theme   = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
  )








Both panels use the same viridis palette and direction so the colours are directly comparable. Areas with many tows by small vessels can show high FishingH but modest KWfishingH; a few large industrial vessels can dominate KWfishingH while contributing fewer hours.






NoteExercise



Work through these steps in order:

Subset. Filter the OSPAR layer to the North Sea (roughly −3° to 9°E, 51° to 57°N) using st_crop(). Recreate the two-panel patchwork for this subregion. Does the spatial pattern look different at this finer scale?
Extend. The select() call in the data-loading chunk above drops FishingH. Add it back, then add a third panel showing FishingH on the same log1p / viridis scale. Use plot_layout(ncol = 3) to arrange all three panels in a single row.
Reflect. The two original panels used different viridis options (plasma vs inferno) in the original file. Both now use "viridis". What was the problem with using different palettes for a side-by-side comparison, and when might deliberately different palettes be appropriate?

title: Simple features
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)

title: Simple features
text:
Geometry types
The seven most common simple feature geometry types are:



Type
Description




POINT
A single location


LINESTRING
A sequence of points connected by straight lines


POLYGON
A closed ring of points forming an area


MULTIPOINT
A set of points as a single feature


MULTILINESTRING
A set of linestrings as a single feature


MULTIPOLYGON
A set of polygons as a single feature


GEOMETRYCOLLECTION
A mix of any geometry types




Additional types exist in the standard (CIRCULARSTRING, SURFACE, TRIANGLE, etc.) but are not supported by the sf package and rarely encountered in practice.

title: Simple features
text:
What is a list column?
A regular data frame column holds a vector of values of the same type. A list column holds a list — each element can be a different type or length. This is how sf stores geometry: each row’s geometry (which might be a simple point or a complex multipolygon with many coordinates) is stored as one element of a list column.

# A regular data frame is itself a list of equal-length vectors
my_df <- data.frame(a = 7:9, b = c("a", "b", "c"), c = c(10, 20, 30))
is.list(my_df)   # TRUE

[1] TRUE

# A list column holds a list as one of those vectors
my_list <- list(1:2, "Hello", c("A", "B", "C"))
my_df$d <- my_list
print(my_df)

  a b  c       d
1 7 a 10    1, 2
2 8 b 20   Hello
3 9 c 30 A, B, C


The geometry column in an sf object works exactly this way — each element is the sfg geometry for that feature.

title: Simple features
text:
Inspecting an sf object
When you print an sf object, the header shows the key metadata before the data:
